content.explanation:
Object types are one of the most frequently used type constructs in TypeScript. They describe the shape of an object — what properties it has and what types those properties hold. The simplest form is an inline object type written directly in a type annotation: `{ name: string; age: number }`. Every property is declared with its name, a colon, and its type, separated by semicolons or commas.

Optional properties are marked with a `?` after the property name: `{ name: string; age?: number }`. An optional property can be omitted entirely when creating the object, and its type becomes a union with `undefined`. This is essential for modeling real-world data where not all fields are always present — API responses, form data, configuration objects, and user preferences commonly have optional fields.

The `readonly` modifier prevents a property from being reassigned after the object is created: `{ readonly id: number; name: string }`. This is a compile-time check only — it doesn't affect runtime behavior. Readonly properties are useful for identifiers, timestamps, and any data that should be set once and never modified. Note that `readonly` is shallow: if a readonly property holds an object, the nested object's properties can still be modified unless they are also marked readonly.

TypeScript uses structural typing for objects, meaning two objects are compatible if they have the same shape, regardless of how they were declared. If a function expects `{ name: string }`, you can pass any object that has a `name` property of type `string`, even if it has additional properties. This is fundamentally different from nominal typing (used in Java/C#) where objects must be explicitly declared as implementing an interface.

codeExamples.title: Inline object types and optional properties
codeExamples.explanation: Optional properties are marked with `?`. When omitted, their value is `undefined`. Always check for undefined before using optional properties.

codeExamples.title: Readonly properties
codeExamples.output:
Alice Smith (id: ${RANDOM})
Name updated: Alice Smith
codeExamples.explanation: Readonly properties cannot be reassigned after creation. Use readonly for IDs, timestamps, and any value that should be immutable. Mutable properties like name can still be updated.

codeExamples.title: Nested object types
codeExamples.output:
123 Main St, Springfield, IL 62701
456 Oak Ave, Portland, OR 97201 (45.52, -122.68)
codeExamples.explanation: Object types can be nested — an optional `coordinates` property contains its own object type with lat and lng. Check the outer optional before accessing nested properties.

notes: TypeScript uses structural typing — objects are compatible based on shape, not by name or declaration.
Use `?` for optional properties and `readonly` for immutable properties — these are the two most common property modifiers.
For inline object types used in more than one place, extract them into an interface or type alias to avoid duplication.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to check optional properties for undefined — accessing `obj.optional.nested` without checking `obj.optional` first causes a runtime error.
Assuming `readonly` is deep — `readonly` only prevents reassignment of the property itself. Nested objects can still be mutated unless their properties are also readonly.

exercises.title: Product with optional fields
exercises.description: Write a function `formatProduct` that takes a product object with required `name` (string) and `price` (number), and optional `category` (string) and `inStock` (boolean, default assumption true). Return a formatted string.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Laptop [Electronics] - $999.99 (In Stock)
Book - $12.50 (In Stock)
Phone - $699.00 (Out of Stock)
hints: Use `?` to mark category and inStock as optional properties in the object type.
Check `inStock === false` explicitly since undefined (omitted) should default to 'In Stock'.

exercises.title: Nested configuration object
exercises.description: Write a function `describeServer` that takes a server config object with `host` (string), `port` (number), and an optional `ssl` object containing `enabled` (boolean) and `certPath` (string).
hints: The ssl property is optional and contains a nested object type: `ssl?: { enabled: boolean; certPath: string }`.
Use optional chaining (`config.ssl?.enabled`) to safely access nested optional properties.

content.explanation:
Interfaces are named object type definitions in TypeScript. They describe the shape of an object by listing its properties and their types. The syntax uses the `interface` keyword: `interface User { name: string; age: number; }`. Unlike inline object types, interfaces are reusable, can be extended, and support declaration merging — making them the primary tool for defining object shapes in TypeScript.

Interfaces support all the property modifiers available for object types: optional properties (`?`), readonly properties (`readonly`), and method signatures. A method can be declared in shorthand: `greet(name: string): string` or as a property with a function type: `greet: (name: string) => string`. Both are equivalent in practice, though the shorthand form is more conventional in interfaces.

Index signatures allow an interface to describe objects with dynamic keys. The syntax is `[key: string]: ValueType`, which means the object can have any string key mapping to the specified value type. This is useful for dictionaries, caches, and configuration objects where the exact keys aren't known in advance. You can combine index signatures with known properties, but the known properties must be compatible with the index signature's value type.

Interfaces are open, meaning they can be declared multiple times with the same name and TypeScript automatically merges them. This is called declaration merging. It's particularly useful for extending third-party types — you can add properties to an existing interface from a library without modifying the library's source code. This feature is unique to interfaces and is one of the key differences from type aliases.

codeExamples.title: Basic interface with methods
codeExamples.explanation: Interfaces combine property definitions and method signatures. The `readonly` modifier on `id` prevents reassignment. Optional `age` can be omitted. The method `getDisplayName()` uses shorthand syntax.

codeExamples.title: Index signatures
codeExamples.explanation: Index signatures allow dynamic keys. StringDictionary accepts any string key with string values. ScoreBoard combines an index signature with a known `gameId` property.

codeExamples.title: Declaration merging
codeExamples.output:
MyApp v1.0.0
Debug: true, Log: info
codeExamples.explanation: Declaration merging automatically combines multiple interface declarations with the same name. This is unique to interfaces — type aliases cannot merge. It's useful for extending library types.

notes: Interfaces are the standard way to define object shapes in TypeScript — use them for any object type that's referenced in multiple places.
Index signatures (`[key: string]: Type`) describe objects with dynamic keys. All known properties must be compatible with the index signature type.
Declaration merging is unique to interfaces — it lets you extend third-party types without modifying their source code.
commonPitfalls: Adding a known property that conflicts with the index signature type — if you have `[key: string]: number`, all properties must be numbers, including known ones.
Expecting type aliases to support declaration merging — only interfaces merge. If you need merging, use an interface.

exercises.title: Define a Todo interface
exercises.description: Create a `Todo` interface with `id` (readonly number), `title` (string), `completed` (boolean), and optional `dueDate` (string). Write a function to format todos.
hints: Use `readonly` on `id` and `?` on `dueDate` to make it optional.
Check for `todo.dueDate` before including it in the output string.

exercises.title: Dictionary with index signature
exercises.description: Create a `WordCount` interface with an index signature for string keys mapping to numbers. Write a function `countWords` that takes a sentence and returns a WordCount object.
hints: Define the interface with `[word: string]: number` as the index signature.
Split the sentence by spaces, then iterate and count each word using the index signature.

content.explanation:
Type aliases create a name for any type using the `type` keyword. While interfaces can only describe object shapes, type aliases can name any type: primitives, unions, intersections, tuples, functions, and more. The syntax is `type MyType = ...`. Type aliases are one of TypeScript's most versatile features and are essential for expressing complex type relationships.

Union types, written with the `|` operator, represent a value that could be one of several types: `type Result = string | number`. A variable of a union type can hold any of the constituent types. To use type-specific operations, you must narrow the type first using type guards like `typeof`, `instanceof`, or property checks. Unions are everywhere in TypeScript — optional properties are unions with `undefined`, nullable values are unions with `null`, and API responses often use union types to represent different outcomes.

Intersection types, written with the `&` operator, combine multiple types into one. `type Admin = User & { permissions: string[] }` creates a type that has all properties of `User` plus a `permissions` array. Intersections are the type-level equivalent of "and" — the resulting type must satisfy all constituent types. They're commonly used to add properties to existing types, combine mixins, and build complex types from simpler ones.

Discriminated unions are a powerful pattern that combines union types with a shared literal property (the discriminant). Each member of the union has a common property with a different literal value: `type Shape = { kind: "circle"; radius: number } | { kind: "square"; side: number }`. By checking the discriminant (`shape.kind`), TypeScript narrows the type to the specific member, enabling safe access to member-specific properties. This pattern is the TypeScript way to implement tagged unions or algebraic data types.

codeExamples.title: Union types and type narrowing
codeExamples.explanation: Union types are narrowed using type guards. `typeof` narrows primitives, while checking a boolean property narrows object unions. After narrowing, TypeScript knows the exact type.

codeExamples.title: Intersection types
codeExamples.output:
Alice (30) - alice@company.com
Role: admin, Perms: read, write, delete
codeExamples.explanation: Intersections combine types with `&`. A `Person` has all properties from HasName, HasEmail, and HasAge. `Admin` extends Person with additional properties.

codeExamples.title: Discriminated unions
codeExamples.explanation: The `kind` property is the discriminant. When you check `shape.kind === 'circle'`, TypeScript narrows the type and knows `shape.radius` exists. This pattern provides exhaustive type-safe handling.

notes: Type aliases can name any type (unions, intersections, tuples, functions), while interfaces are limited to object shapes.
Union types (`|`) mean "either this or that" — use type guards to narrow before accessing type-specific properties.
Discriminated unions use a shared literal property as a tag to enable safe type narrowing in switch statements.
commonPitfalls: Accessing union-specific properties without narrowing first — `(value: string | number).toUpperCase()` is an error because `number` doesn't have that method.
Creating intersection types with conflicting properties — `{ x: string } & { x: number }` produces `{ x: never }` since no value can be both string and number.

exercises.title: API response type
exercises.description: Define a discriminated union type `ApiResponse` with two variants: a success response with `status: "ok"` and `data: string`, and an error response with `status: "error"` and `message: string`. Write a function to handle both cases.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Success: User data loaded
Failed: Not found
hints: Use a discriminated union with `status` as the discriminant: one variant has `"ok"`, the other has `"error"`.
Use a switch on `response.status` — TypeScript narrows the type in each case branch.

exercises.title: Composing types with intersections
exercises.description: Define base types `Timestamped` (with `createdAt` and `updatedAt` strings) and `Identifiable` (with `id` number). Create a `Post` type that intersects both with `{ title: string; body: string }`. Write a function to format a post.
exercises.expectedOutput:
[#1] Hello World (created: 2026-01-15, updated: 2026-01-16)
This is my first post.
hints: Define each base type separately, then use `&` to combine them: `type Post = Identifiable & Timestamped & { ... }`.
The resulting Post type has all properties from all three types.

content.explanation:
Interface extension is TypeScript's mechanism for building complex interfaces from simpler ones. Using the `extends` keyword, a new interface inherits all properties from one or more parent interfaces and can add new properties or override existing ones. The syntax is `interface Child extends Parent { ... }`. This is the interface equivalent of the intersection type (`&`), but with a more explicit inheritance relationship.

A key advantage of `extends` over intersections is error detection. If you accidentally declare a property in the child interface that conflicts with the parent, TypeScript immediately shows an error. With intersection types, conflicting properties silently produce `never`, which can be confusing to debug. The explicit inheritance of `extends` makes the relationship clear and catches conflicts early.

TypeScript supports multiple interface inheritance: `interface AdminUser extends User, Permissions { ... }`. The child interface inherits properties from all listed parents and can add its own. If multiple parents have properties with the same name, those properties must have compatible types. This is similar to multiple inheritance in other languages but applied only to type structure, not implementation.

Interface extension is widely used in real-world TypeScript for modeling domain hierarchies, API response types, and component prop types. In React, for example, you might have a `BaseButtonProps` interface extended by `IconButtonProps` and `LinkButtonProps`. ORMs like Prisma use interface extension to build model types. The pattern keeps types DRY and makes the relationships between types explicit and self-documenting.

codeExamples.title: Basic interface extension
codeExamples.explanation: Pet extends Animal, inheriting `name` and `sound` while adding `owner` and `vaccinated`. Every Pet is also a valid Animal, but not every Animal is a Pet.

codeExamples.title: Multiple interface extension
codeExamples.explanation: LogEntry extends three interfaces simultaneously. It must implement all inherited methods (toString, toJSON) and properties (logLevel), plus its own (timestamp, message).

codeExamples.title: Building a type hierarchy
codeExamples.explanation: A three-level hierarchy: BaseEntity -> User -> Employee. Each level adds properties. Employee has all properties from the chain: id, createdAt, name, email, department, and salary.

notes: Use `extends` for clear inheritance relationships — it's more readable than intersection types for object hierarchies.
Multiple inheritance is supported: `interface C extends A, B { }` — all parent properties must be compatible.
Child interfaces can add new properties but cannot remove inherited ones. They can narrow property types but not widen them.
commonPitfalls: Extending interfaces with conflicting property types — if Parent has `name: string` and you try to redeclare `name: number` in the child, TypeScript shows an error.
Creating overly deep inheritance chains — more than 3-4 levels becomes hard to follow. Prefer composition (using properties of interface types) over deep inheritance.

exercises.title: Vehicle hierarchy
exercises.description: Create a `Vehicle` interface with `make` (string) and `year` (number). Extend it to create `Car` (adds `doors`: number) and `Motorcycle` (adds `engineCC`: number). Write a function to describe each.
hints: Both Car and Motorcycle extend Vehicle. Use `extends` keyword for each.
Use `"doors" in vehicle` to narrow the union type and determine if it's a Car or Motorcycle.

exercises.title: Composable event system
exercises.description: Create a `BaseEvent` interface (with `type`: string and `timestamp`: string). Extend it to create `ClickEvent` (adds `x`, `y` numbers) and `KeyEvent` (adds `key`: string, `ctrlKey`: boolean). Write a handler function.
hints: Narrow the `type` property to a literal in each child interface to create a discriminated union.
Check `event.type` in the handler to narrow to the specific event type and access its unique properties.

content.explanation:
One of the most common TypeScript questions — in interviews and in practice — is when to use `type` versus `interface`. Both can describe object shapes, and for simple cases they're interchangeable. However, they have distinct capabilities that make each more appropriate in specific situations. Understanding these differences helps you make the right choice and write idiomatic TypeScript.

Interfaces have two unique capabilities: declaration merging and `extends` syntax. Declaration merging means you can declare the same interface name multiple times and TypeScript combines them automatically. This is essential for augmenting third-party library types. The `extends` syntax provides clear, explicit inheritance and catches property conflicts at the point of declaration. Interfaces also have slightly better error messages when complex object types don't match.

Type aliases have broader capabilities: they can represent unions (`A | B`), intersections (`A & B`), tuples (`[string, number]`), mapped types, conditional types, and primitive type aliases (`type ID = string`). Interfaces cannot express any of these. If you need a union type, a tuple type, a function type alias, or any computed/mapped type, you must use `type`. This makes type aliases more versatile overall.

The practical guideline used by most teams: use `interface` for defining the shapes of objects and classes (especially public APIs that others might need to extend), and use `type` for everything else — unions, intersections, utility types, function types, and any type that isn't purely an object shape. Some teams prefer a simpler rule: always use `type` unless you specifically need declaration merging. Either convention works as long as the team is consistent.

codeExamples.title: Interface-only features
codeExamples.explanation: Declaration merging is unique to interfaces — declaring the same interface name twice merges them. The `extends` keyword provides explicit inheritance with conflict detection.

codeExamples.title: Type alias-only features
codeExamples.explanation: Type aliases can express unions, tuples, mapped types, and function types — none of which are possible with interfaces. These are the cases where `type` is required.

codeExamples.title: Practical guidelines: when to use each
codeExamples.explanation: Use interfaces for object shapes (UserProfile) and type aliases for everything else: unions (UserRole), intersections (UserWithRole), and utility types (UserKeys). This is the most common convention.

notes: Use `interface` for object shapes and public APIs that might be extended. Use `type` for unions, tuples, mapped types, and non-object types.
Only interfaces support declaration merging — this is essential for extending third-party library types.
Both `interface extends` and `type &` can combine object types, but `extends` provides better error messages for conflicts.
commonPitfalls: Using interfaces for everything, even unions and tuples — interfaces cannot express these. Use type aliases for non-object types.
Relying on declaration merging accidentally — if you declare two interfaces with the same name in the same scope, they merge silently, which can cause unexpected type additions.
interviewTips: Key differences to mention: interfaces support declaration merging and `extends`, while type aliases support unions, intersections, tuples, and mapped types. For object shapes, both work; for everything else, use type.

A strong answer includes the practical guideline: use `interface` for object contracts (especially public APIs) and `type` for computed types, unions, and utilities. Mention that consistency within a team matters more than the specific choice.

exercises.title: Choose the right tool
exercises.description: Model a notification system: use an interface for the base `Notification` shape (id, message, read), a type alias for `NotificationType` union ("info" | "warning" | "error"), and combine them properly. Write a function to format notifications.
hints: Use `type` for the string literal union (NotificationType) and `interface` for the object shape (Notification).
Use the type alias as a property type within the interface: `type: NotificationType`.

exercises.title: Extensible API types
exercises.description: Create a base `ApiRequest` interface with `url` (string) and `method` (string). Use interface extension to create `GetRequest` and `PostRequest`. Use a type alias to create a union `Request` type. Write a function that handles both request types.
hints: Use interface extension for GetRequest and PostRequest. Narrow the `method` property to a literal type in each child.
Use a type alias for the union: `type Request = GetRequest | PostRequest`. The narrowed `method` property acts as a discriminant.
